symbols: dragon at Margaret's feet — the devil she conquered, her triumph over evil
rod or cross held by Margaret — the instrument of her victory over the dragon
pincers held by Apollonia — the instrument of her martyrdom (teeth pulled out)
radiant halos — the glory of sainthood and heavenly favor
calm expressions — the peace of those who have overcome through faith
description: Two standing female saints occupy the panel in a Gothic interior. At left, a woman in a red dress and dark blue mantle with a radiant gold halo holds a thin rod or small cross. At her feet, a dark dragon-like creature is visible. At right, a woman in a deep purple robe with gold trim and a radiant halo holds what appears to be a small pair of pincers or tongs. Both women have long hair and serene, downward-gazing expressions. The background shows a stone interior with an arched window.
theological_description: Rogier van der Weyden depicts two popular female virgin martyrs of the early Church. Saint Margaret of Antioch, shown with the dragon, legendarily was swallowed by a dragon (the devil) but burst free through the power of the cross she carried. Her story dramatizes the promise of Genesis 3:15, where the seed of the woman will crush the serpent's head. Saint Apollonia of Alexandria had her teeth violently extracted during persecution before being burned, and she holds the pincers as her identifying attribute. Both saints exemplify the New Testament teaching that believers may overcome evil through faith: 'they have conquered him by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony, for they loved not their lives even unto death' (Rev 12:11). Their serene countenances reflect the Pauline conviction that 'neither death nor life... will be able to separate us from the love of God' (Rom 8:38-39).

scripture_refs.reason: They conquered by the blood of the Lamb — martyrs who did not love their lives unto death
The seed of the woman shall crush the serpent — Margaret triumphing over the dragon
Nothing can separate us from the love of God — the saints' serene confidence
I have fought the good fight — the crown of righteousness for the faithful
